narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure automatically triggers a rejection to ensure the program's overall success rates remain strong. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar exam struggles compared to the much higher average deciles—around the 6th out of 10—for students who passed. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, far below the typical mid-to-upper rankings seen among successful bar takers. Interestingly, your LSAT score stood out positively as the third-most influential element, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it was reasonably competitive, though still below the average of 37.27 for those who succeeded. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and middle family income bracket played roles, they weren't the top drivers here, and your choice to attend full-time aligns well with the vast majority of passers. Overall, your early academic deciles painted a picture riskier than the profiles of accepted students, who generally showed stronger consistent performance from the start. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal strengths, but rather how these patterns statistically link to bar outcomes in our data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence or strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve future decile-like metrics. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as many students in similar situations have turned things around with focused effort. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view in your favor, and we're here to discuss options if you'd like. Your determination will make the difference.
